ir_specs.statement: The chmod command changes the access permissions of the named files.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chmod changes access permissions of named files.

ir_specs.statement: The chmod command does not change the permissions of symbolic links on most systems.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chmod usually does not change symlink permissions on most systems.

ir_specs.statement: For each symbolic link listed on the command line, chmod changes the permissions of the pointed-to file.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: When a symlink is a command-line argument, chmod affects the file it points to.

ir_specs.statement: The chmod command ignores symbolic links encountered during recursive directory traversals.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: During recursive traversal, chmod ignores encountered symbolic links unless traversal options change the behavior.

ir_specs.statement: Only a process whose effective user ID matches the user ID of the file, or a process with appropriate privileges, may change the file mode bits.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: Changing file mode bits is limited to the owner-equivalent effective user ID or a privileged process.

ir_specs.statement: The chmod --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default for command line arguments.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chmod --dereference affects referents and is the default for symlink command-line arguments.

ir_specs.statement: The chmod --reference option changes each file's mode to be the same as that of the reference file.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chmod --reference copies mode bits from a reference file.

ir_specs.statement: The chmod --recursive option recursively changes permissions of directories and their contents.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chmod --recursive applies permission changes through directories and their contents.

ir_specs.statement: With recursive chmod, the -H option traverses a command line argument that is a symbolic link to a directory, and this is the default if none of -H, -L, or -P is specified.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: For recursive chmod, -H follows command-line symlinks to directories and is the default traversal mode.

ir_specs.statement: With recursive chmod, the -L option traverses every symbolic link to a directory that is encountered.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: In recursive mode, chmod -L follows every encountered symlink to a directory.

ir_specs.statement: With recursive chmod, the -P option does not traverse any symbolic links.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: In recursive mode, chmod -P avoids traversing symlinks.

ir_specs.statement: The chmod --preserve-root option fails upon attempts to recursively change the root directory '/'.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chmod --preserve-root protects '/' from recursive permission changes.

ir_specs.statement: The chown command changes the user and/or group ownership of each given file.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chown changes user ownership, group ownership, or both.

ir_specs.statement: If chown is given owner:group, the group ownership of the files is changed as well.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: The owner:group form changes both owner and group.

ir_specs.statement: If a colon but no group name follows owner, chown changes the group of the files to the owner's login group.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: The owner: form sets the group to the owner's login group.

ir_specs.statement: If chown is given :group with the owner omitted, only the group of the files is changed, and chown performs the same function as chgrp.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: The :group form changes only group ownership and behaves like chgrp.

ir_specs.statement: If only a colon is given, or if new-owner is empty, neither the owner nor the group is changed.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: An empty chown owner/group specification changes nothing.

ir_specs.statement: The chown --from option changes a file's ownership only if it has the current attributes specified by old-owner.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chown --from adds a current-owner precondition to the change.

ir_specs.statement: The chown --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default when not operating recursively.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: For non-recursive chown, dereferencing symlinks is the default.

ir_specs.statement: The chown --no-dereference option acts on symbolic links themselves instead of what they point to.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chown --no-dereference targets the symlink itself.

ir_specs.statement: The chown --reference option changes the user and group of each file to match those of the reference file, using the referent if the reference is a symbolic link.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chown --reference copies owner and group from a reference target, not from a symlink object.

ir_specs.statement: The chown --recursive option recursively changes ownership of directories and their contents.
ir_specs.source_section: 13.1 chown: Change file owner and group
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chown --recursive applies through directories and their contents.

ir_specs.statement: The chgrp command changes the group ownership of each given file or uses the group of an existing reference file.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chgrp changes group ownership directly or from a reference file.

ir_specs.statement: If group is intended to represent a numeric group ID, chgrp allows it to be specified with a leading '+'.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: GNU chgrp allows a leading plus sign to disambiguate numeric group IDs.

ir_specs.statement: The chgrp --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default when not operating recursively.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: For non-recursive chgrp, dereferencing symlinks is the default.

ir_specs.statement: The chgrp --reference option changes each file's group to match the reference file, using the referent if the reference is a symbolic link.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chgrp --reference copies group ownership from the reference target, not from a symlink object.

ir_specs.statement: The chgrp --recursive option recursively changes the group ownership of directories and their contents.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: chgrp --recursive applies through directories and their contents.

ir_specs.statement: With recursive chgrp, the -P option does not traverse any symbolic links, and this is the default if none of -H, -L, or -P is specified.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: For recursive chgrp, -P means no symlink traversal and is the default traversal mode.

ir_specs.statement: File mode bits control the kinds of access that users have to a file.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: File mode bits define what kinds of access users have to a file.

ir_specs.statement: File mode bits can be represented either in symbolic form or as an octal number.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: GNU Coreutils describes file mode bits in symbolic form and octal form.

question_specs.question: The `chmod` command changes the access permissions of named files.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chmod command modifies the access permissions of the named files.

question_specs.question: On most systems, `chmod` directly changes the permissions stored on a symbolic link itself.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chmod command does not change the permissions of symbolic links on most systems.

question_specs.question: When a symbolic link is listed on the command line, `chmod` changes the permissions of the file it points to.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. For each symbolic link listed on the command line, chmod changes the permissions of the pointed-to file.

question_specs.question: During recursive directory traversals, `chmod` always changes the permissions of every symbolic link it encounters.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chmod command ignores symbolic links encountered during recursive directory traversals.

question_specs.question: Any process can change the mode bits of any file as long as the path exists.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. Only a process whose effective user ID matches the user ID of the file, or a process with appropriate privileges, may change the file mode bits.

question_specs.question: For command-line symlink arguments, `chmod --dereference` matches the default behavior.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chmod --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default for command line arguments.

question_specs.question: The `chmod --reference` option copies the mode from a reference file.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chmod --reference option changes each file's mode to be the same as that of the reference file.

question_specs.question: The `chmod --recursive` option changes only the top directory and never touches its contents.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chmod --recursive option recursively changes permissions of directories and their contents.

question_specs.question: With recursive `chmod`, `-H` can traverse a command-line argument that is a symlink to a directory.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. With recursive chmod, the -H option traverses a command line argument that is a symbolic link to a directory, and this is the default if none of -H, -L, or -P is specified.

question_specs.question: With recursive `chmod`, `-L` refuses to traverse symlinks to directories that are encountered.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. With recursive chmod, the -L option traverses every symbolic link to a directory that is encountered.

question_specs.question: With recursive `chmod`, the `-P` option means symbolic links are not traversed.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. With recursive chmod, the -P option does not traverse any symbolic links.

question_specs.question: The `chmod --preserve-root` option has no effect when a recursive `chmod` operation targets `/`.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chmod --preserve-root option fails upon attempts to recursively change the root directory '/'.

question_specs.question: The `chown` command can change user ownership, group ownership, or both.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chown command modifies the user and/or group ownership of each given file.

question_specs.question: If `chown` is given `owner:group`, it changes only the owner and leaves the group unchanged.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. If chown is given owner:group, the group ownership of the files is changed as well.

question_specs.question: If `chown` is given `owner:` with no group name, the file's group is changed to that owner's login group.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. If a colon but no group name follows owner, chown changes the group of the files to the owner's login group.

question_specs.question: If `chown` is given `:group` with no owner, it changes only the group and acts like `chgrp`.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. If chown is given :group with the owner omitted, only the group of the files is changed, and chown performs the same function as chgrp.

question_specs.question: If `chown` is given only `:` or an empty new-owner, it resets both owner and group to defaults.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. If only a colon is given, or if new-owner is empty, neither the owner nor the group is changed.

question_specs.question: The `chown --from` option changes ownership only when the file currently has the specified old-owner attributes.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chown --from option changes a file's ownership only if it has the current attributes specified by old-owner.

question_specs.question: When `chown` is not operating recursively, acting on what a symbolic link points to is the default behavior.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chown --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default when not operating recursively.

question_specs.question: The `chown --no-dereference` option always acts on the file pointed to by a symbolic link.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chown --no-dereference option acts on symbolic links themselves instead of what they point to.

question_specs.question: If the reference file is a symbolic link, `chown --reference` uses the owner and group of the symlink itself.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chown --reference option changes the user and group of each file to match those of the reference file, using the referent if the reference is a symbolic link.

question_specs.question: The `chown --recursive` option recursively changes ownership of directories and their contents.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chown --recursive option recursively changes ownership of directories and their contents.

question_specs.question: The `chgrp` command can change a file's user ownership directly when you pass a user name.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chgrp command modifies the group ownership of each given file or uses the group of an existing reference file.

question_specs.question: GNU `chgrp` forbids using a leading `+` when a numeric group ID is intended.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. If group is intended to represent a numeric group ID, chgrp allows it to be specified with a leading '+'.

question_specs.question: When `chgrp` is not operating recursively, dereferencing symbolic links is the default behavior.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chgrp --dereference option acts on what symbolic links point to, and this is the default when not operating recursively.

question_specs.question: If the reference file is a symbolic link, `chgrp --reference` uses the group of the symlink itself rather than the file it refers to.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. chgrp --reference option changes each file's group to match the reference file, using the referent if the reference is a symbolic link.

question_specs.question: The `chgrp --recursive` option recursively changes the group ownership of directories and their contents.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. chgrp --recursive option recursively changes the group ownership of directories and their contents.

question_specs.question: With recursive `chgrp`, `-P` means every symlink to a directory will be traversed, and this is the default traversal mode.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. With recursive chgrp, the -P option does not traverse any symbolic links, and this is the default if none of -H, -L, or -P is specified.

question_specs.question: File mode bits control the kinds of access that users have to a file.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. File mode bits control the kinds of access that users have to a file.

question_specs.question: File mode bits can be represented only in symbolic form and not as octal numbers.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. File mode bits can be represented either in symbolic form or as an octal number.
